%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Section Objective:** Memorize a small set of mathematical constants that appear repeatedly in signals and systems work — so you can use them instantly without re-derivation.

Section B.8-1 is a compact reference listing the numerical values of the most frequently used mathematical constants: π, ε, ε⁻¹, λογ₁₀ 2, and λογ₁₀ 3. These constants appear throughout signal analysis, Fourier transforms, and system calculations. After studying this section, you will be able to recall these values to sufficient decimal precision, recognize when a calculation requires them, and avoid the common exam trap of confusing ε with π or misremembering ε⁻¹.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 The five key mathematical constants from B.8-1, formatted as a quick-reference table with an exam-tip callout for the most commonly confused value.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777218034370-2200.png)

## Core Idea

The central relationship in B.8-1 is simple but easy to underestimate: **a small fixed set of constants recurs constantly across all of signals and systems**, and knowing their decimal values to 4-5 significant figures lets you work quickly and accurately.

The five constants are:

| Constant | Value |
|----------|-------|
| \(\pi\) | \(\approx 3.14159\) |
| \(e\) | \(\approx 2.71828\) |
| \(1/e\) | \(\approx 0.36788\) |
| \(\log_{10} 2\) | \(\approx 0.30103\) |
| \(\log_{10} 3\) | \(\approx 0.47712\) |

### KEY INSIGHT

\(1/e\) is **not** a new constant — it is simply \(e^{-1}\). But because it appears so often in exponential decay problems (e.g., the time constant \(\tau\) of a decaying signal), it is listed separately for convenience.

### COMMON MISTAKE

Students frequently confuse \(e \approx 2.718\) with \(1/e \approx 0.368\). Remember: \(1/e < 1\) because \(e > 1\). If your answer gives a "decay" value greater than 1, you have likely used \(e\) where you needed \(1/e\).

**Minimal example:** If a signal decays as \(x(t) = e^{-t}\), then at \(t = 1\) the value is \(e^{-1} \approx 0.368\) — roughly 37% of the original amplitude.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Five constants — \(\pi\), \(e\), \(1/e\), \(\log_{10} 2\), \(\log_{10} 3\) — recur throughout signals and systems work.
- \(1/e \approx 0.368\) is \(e^{-1}\); it is less than 1 and governs exponential decay at one time constant.
- Do not confuse \(e \approx 2.718\) with \(1/e \approx 0.368\) — they differ by nearly a factor of 7.

*With these constants in hand, the next section B.8-2 builds on them directly, using \(e\) and \(\pi\) to define the complex exponential identities that are the backbone of Fourier and Laplace analysis.*
